title: Interfacial Coupling and Sparse Intercalation of 7-Atom-Wide Armchair Graphene Nanoribbons by N-Heterocyclic Carbene Monolayers
authors: Dominik Lüthi, Lin Yang, Xiuling Yu, Ji Ma, Xinliang Feng, Carlo A. Pignedoli, Roman Fasel, Gabriela Borin Barin
abstract: Graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) synthesized on metal substrates experience electronic coupling and screening from the underlying surface, which, although often weak, can modify their observed properties and complicate their transfer to device-compatible substrates. Intercalation of GNRs by self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) offers a possible route to reduce this interaction. Here, we investigate the intercalation of 7-atom-wide armchair graphene nanoribbons (7-AGNRs) on Au(111) using N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs). Low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations reveal that the adsorption geometry of the NHCs strongly influences the intercalation yield for GNRs. Methyl-substituted NHCs form flat-lying dimers that partially intercalate the GNRs, producing locally decoupled segments. In contrast, bulkier isopropyl-substituted NHCs form upright monomers that embed the GNRs within the monolayer, preventing intercalation. The low intercalation yield indicates that lifting the nanoribbon from the Au surface is energetically costly. These results establish molecular adsorption geometry and packing as key parameters controlling intercalation at GNR-metal interfaces, with implications for the rational design of decoupling layers for GNR-based device integration pathways.
summary: This research explores how N-heterocyclic carbene monolayers can be used to decouple Graphene Nanoribbons from metal substrates. By comparing methyl and isopropyl derivatives, the authors show that the molecule's physical size and adsorption geometry critically determine whether intercalation occurs, leading to electronically decoupled segments favorable for device fabrication.
main_problem: The study investigates methods to electronically decouple Graphene Nanoribbons (GNRs) from their metallic growth substrates, which is crucial for device integration.
main_result: The adsorption geometry of N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs) dictates the intercalation outcome; methyl-substituted NHCs ($	ext{NHCMe}$) can partially intercalate and electronically decouple the GNRs, while bulkier isopropyl groups ($	ext{NHCiPr}$) prevent this.
method: The research combines low-temperature STM/STS measurements to probe electronic structure with DFT calculations and Raman spectroscopy to analyze interfacial coupling mechanisms.
model_or_system: The system involves 7-atom-wide armchair graphene nanoribbons (7-AGNRs) placed on Au(111), which are subsequently treated with self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) formed by $	ext{NHCMe}$ or $	ext{NHCiPr}$.
key_observables: Electronic band gap size and symmetry (STS); Raman mode shifts ($	ext{G}$ and RBLM); Intercalation yield percentage; DFT calculated relative binding energies ($\Delta E$).
important_parameters: Molecular adsorption geometry, NHC ligand bulkiness, temperature (especially low T for STM), and the energy cost of lifting the GNR from the metal surface.
main_assumptions: The interpretation relies on distinguishing between true intercalation, simple top-adsorption, and embedded states; thermal effects in Raman spectroscopy must be carefully separated from chemical changes.
figures_summary: Figures show STM images detailing SAM structures ($	ext{NHCMe}$ dimers vs. $	ext{NHCiPr}$ upright monomers); STS spectra compare gaps on bare Au(111) versus intercalated GNRs; DFT results map out energy barriers for intercalation.
paper_structure: The paper systematically characterizes the NHC SAM formation (using STM/NMR), then uses low-temperature STM/STS to distinguish between various adsorption states, followed by DFT and Raman analysis to provide theoretical and spectroscopic evidence supporting the geometric control over electronic decoupling.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed case study in controlling interfacial electronic coupling using molecular engineering, which is highly relevant for designing functional quantum materials interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2: STM images related to attempts at intercalating 7-AGNRs with NHCs on Au(111). a 7-AGNRs grown on Au(111) by on-surface bottom-up synthesis under UHV conditions, imaged at 4 K. b STM image acquired at room-temperature after deposition of NHCMe onto a 7-AGNR- covered surface. Ribbon-like features (as highlighted in blue) are visible and appear to be located above the NHCMe SAM, suggesting intercalation. c Similarly prepared sample imaged at 4 K. GNR-like features (as highlighted in blue) are again observed, though most appear segmented or discontinuous. d STM image of 7-AGNRs with NHCiPr, acquired at 4 K. The GNRs now appear more clearly embedded within the SAM, with a distinct...

Figure 3: Low-temperature STM analysis of 7-AGNRs combined with NHCMe SAMs on Au(111), revealing multiple interaction motifs. a GNR with adsorbed NHCMe dimers on its surface, evident from the segmented, dimer-like contrast along the ribbon axis. b Sample after mild annealing (below 50 ◦C), showing cleaner embedded GNRs free of adsorbates. c Reference image of a 7- AGNR on bare Au(111). d Close-up showing densely packed NHCMe dimers adsorbed along the GNR edges. e Embedded GNR decorated with densely packed adsorbates on top. f Intercalated GNR at high negative bias, showing the typical valence band contrast of electronically decoupled 7-AGNRs. g Same GNR imaged at lower bias, where the state...

Figure 4: a DFT-optimized geometries of different NHCMe–7-AGNR configurations. I Embedded 7-AGNR in a SAM of six NHCMe dimers on Au(111), serving as the reference configuration. II– V Subsequent configurations illustrate adsorption and intercalation steps as discussed in the main text. b Relative energies (∆E) of relaxed configurations for NHCMe (blue) and NHCiPr (orange), referenced to the embedded structure (emb). “1top”, “2top”, and “3top” denote one, two, or three NHC units adsorbed on top of the ribbon, whereas “1below”, “2below”, and “3below” denote configurations with one, two, or three NHC units placed below the GNR. Figure 5: Raman spectroscopic characterization of 7-AGNRs with and without NHC intercalation or embedding. a Raman spectra acquired using a 532 nm laser (spectral range: 320–1790 cm-1) for: (1) 7-AGNRs on Au(111) (laser power: 10 mW, integration time: 0.5 s), (2) 7-AGNRs with excess NHCMe resulting in partial intercalation (10 mW, 0.5 s), (3) 7-AGNRs embedded in an NHCMe SAM following mild annealing to desorb excess carbenes (20 mW, 0.5 s), and (4) 7- AGNRs embedded in an NHCiPr SAM (2 mW, 1 s). b Close-up of the RBLM region. A shift to higher wavenumbers is observed for samples with NHCMe (2 and 3), while no shift is detected for the NHCiPr case (4). c Close-up of the G-mode region....
